metadata.summary: A framework for designing and operating subscription businesses: choose model type, structure tiers, instrument metrics, automate billing recovery, and manage the full customer lifecycle.

content.title: Subscription Models
content.summary: A framework for designing and operating subscription businesses: choose model type (SaaS, membership, replenishment, curation), structure tiers with strategic feature allocation, instrument MRR/churn/LTV metrics, automate billing recovery (dunning), and manage the full customer lifecycle from trial through win-back. Net Revenue Retention above 100% is the goal — meaning expansion revenue offsets churn.

why.p: One-time sales require constant new acquisition; subscriptions create compounding recurring revenue. The mechanism: annual plans reduce churn (cash in, less friction to cancel), dunning automation recovers 20–40% of failed payments that would otherwise be silent churn, and lifecycle interventions (at-risk triggers, save offers) extend LTV at near-zero marginal cost.

list.item: Launching a SaaS, digital membership, or content platform.
Converting an existing one-time-purchase product to recurring revenue.
Churn rate is above 5%/month and root cause is unclear.
Expansion revenue (upgrades) is zero — missing upsell/tier paths.
Setting up billing infrastructure for the first time (Stripe, Paddle).
One-time high-value services (consulting, agency work) where recurring isn't the model.
Products with naturally episodic demand (event software, seasonal tools).
When product has not yet demonstrated retention past 90 days — subscription without retention is an expensive churn machine.
Physical product subscriptions without reliable supply chain — replenishment model requires fulfillment reliability.

section.name: subscription-model-types
section.p: Five subscription model types with best-fit guidance.

list.item: SaaS — software access. Users pay for ongoing access to a platform or tool. Best for: tools, platforms, productivity software.
Membership — community or content access. Users pay for belonging and exclusive content. Best for: education, professional communities, newsletters.
Replenishment — regular physical product delivery. Best for: consumables, personal care, food.
Curation — curated selections delivered periodically. Best for: discovery products (book clubs, music, fashion boxes).
Access — exclusive availability (not content, but priority or privilege). Best for: premium services, concierge products.

rule.statement: Offer both monthly and annual billing. Present annual as "2 months free" (not "17% off"). For most SaaS, annual reduces churn by 3–5x compared to monthly-only.
rule.rationale: Annual billing creates a 12-month commitment window before the next churn decision. "2 months free" is tangible (users can visualize two months); "17% off" is abstract.

rule.statement: Use Good-Better-Best (max 4 tiers) as default. Free tier must be limited enough that a real user would want to upgrade within 30 days of hitting the limit.
rule.rationale: Too-generous free tiers eliminate upgrade motivation. The most common subscription failure pattern is a free tier that fully satisfies the core use case.

example.title: Good-Better-Best for a SaaS tool
example.code: Basic ($9/mo): Core features, 100 items limit Pro ($29/mo): Full features, unlimited (anchor tier) Team ($79/mo): Collaboration + priority support
example.note: Core value limited on free, full access on Pro, collaboration on Team.

example.title: Usage + Base for API-driven product
example.code: Base ($29/mo): Platform access, 1K API calls included Usage: $0.01 per additional API call
example.note: Base covers fixed costs, usage covers variable.

rule.statement: Track Net Revenue Retention (NRR) as the single health metric. NRR above 100% means expansion revenue exceeds churn; below 100% means the business is leaking faster than it's filling.
rule.rationale: NRR captures both churn and expansion in one number. A business with 8% monthly churn but 12% expansion can grow despite the churn. NRR is the metric investors use to assess subscription business quality.

list.item: MRR = sum of all monthly subscriptions (excluding one-time fees).
ARR = MRR x 12 (for annual planning only; don't track day-to-day in ARR).
Monthly churn rate = customers lost / customers at start of period.
LTV = ARPU / monthly churn rate (higher ARPU and lower churn compound LTV significantly).
LTV:CAC target = 3:1 minimum; 5:1 is healthy.
NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion MRR - Churn MRR) / Starting MRR x 100.

rule.statement: Implement dunning automation before launch: retry failed payments on Day 1, 3, and 7 with accompanying emails; downgrade to free on Day 10. This recovers 20–40% of would-be churned revenue.
rule.rationale: 15–25% of subscription cancellations are involuntary (expired cards, insufficient funds). Without automated recovery, this becomes silent permanent churn. Most customers with failed payments want to keep the subscription.

example.title: 5-step payment recovery flow
example.code: Day 0: Payment fails → log event Day 1: Email (friendly notice) + retry charge Day 3: Email (urgency) + retry charge Day 7: Email (final warning: access ends in 3 days) + retry Day 10: Downgrade to free tier, data retained 30 days Day 30: Win-back campaign email to churned users
example.note: Each step includes email + automatic retry attempt.

antipattern.description: No annual billing option at launch.
antipattern.reason: Annual subscribers churn 3–5x less. Missing annual billing at launch means all early customers are monthly, with no commitment lock-in and no cash flow improvement.

antipattern.description: Making cancellation difficult (multi-step forms, mandatory calls).
antipattern.reason: Difficult cancellation increases chargebacks, generates negative reviews, and is illegal in some jurisdictions. Easy cancellation builds trust and sometimes produces "saved" customers who were testing how painful it is.

rule.statement: Instrument lifecycle stage transitions and trigger interventions at each: onboarding email Day 1, check-in Day 3, conversion offer Day 7 (for trials); usage-drop alert for at-risk; save offer at cancellation; win-back at Day 30 post-churn.
rule.rationale: Each lifecycle stage has a different intervention ROI. Day 1 onboarding email has the highest open rate of any email the user will ever receive (50–70%). At-risk outreach is cheaper than win-back.

list.item: Trial Day 1: welcome email + first value action prompt.
Trial Day 3: check-in email — did they complete the first action? Help if not.
Trial Day 7: conversion offer — limited-time or exclusive bonus for upgrading now.
Active monthly: usage report email — show them the value delivered this month.
At-risk (usage drop 50%+ week-over-week): proactive outreach, offer help session.
Cancel request: save offer (discount, pause, downgrade) before confirming cancellation.
Churned Day 30: win-back campaign — announce new feature or offer re-entry discount.

example.title: Three-tier usage-based pricing; 115% NRR
example.code:
Tiers: Starter $19/mo: 1K contacts, 10K emails/mo Growth $49/mo: 5K contacts, 50K emails/mo (40% of users) Pro $99/mo: 15K contacts, 150K emails/mo
Outcomes: 40% choose Growth (anchor tier) Net Revenue Retention: 115% Monthly churn rate: 3% (Expansion from contact list growth offsets churn)
example.note: Three-tier usage-based pricing; 115% NRR achieved through expansion (users growing their contact lists).

example.title: Freemium + two paid tiers
example.code:
Tiers: Free $0: Public content only Member $29/mo: All courses + community VIP $99/mo: + Live sessions + mentorship
Outcomes: Free-to-paid conversion: 5% Annual retention (paid): 85% VIP = 2% of paid users, 15% of revenue (VIP LTV ~10x Member LTV)
example.note: Freemium + two paid tiers; small VIP tier generates outsized revenue share.

template.purpose: Model design document: type, billing, tiers, metric targets, lifecycle automation plan.
